1.synthesis:
Judges 16:1–3 opens the chapter with a brief scene that functions as both a foreshadowing and a character statement: Samson goes to Gaza — one of the five Philistine cities, the most distant from his Israelite home — and enters the house of a prostitute. The Gazites hear that Samson is there and set an ambush at the city gate, planning to kill him at dawn. But Samson rises at midnight, seizes the doors of the city gate with its posts and bar, 'and put them on his shoulders and carried them to the top of the hill that is in front of Hebron' (v. 3). He walks away with Gaza's gate — the city's primary defensive structure — and deposits it twenty-five miles away at a hill near Hebron.

The episode is presented without editorial comment; the narrator neither condemns the prostitution nor celebrates only the gate-removal. The three-verse unit accomplishes two things simultaneously: it demonstrates that Samson's strength remains fully operative in the depth of Philistine territory (Gaza, not Timnah), and it reveals the pattern that will be exploited in the Delilah episode — Samson sleeping in enemy territory with Philistine women while enemies plot around him. The gate he carries away is a comic humiliation of Philistine military power; the bed he lies in is the site of his eventual undoing.
1.historical_context: Gaza was the southernmost of the five Philistine cities and approximately forty miles from Samson's territory — deep in enemy-controlled land. City gates in the ancient Near East were the nerve center of a city's defense and public life: where elders sat for judgment (Ruth 4:1), where commerce occurred, where the military defense could be sealed. Carrying the gate with its posts and bar — an enormous wooden structure reinforced with metal — to the hill near Hebron (some manuscripts read Hebron itself) is the narrative's most deliberately comic display of Samson's strength. The verb 'carry on his shoulders' (yissa' al shkhemo) is used elsewhere of carrying the ark's poles — a subtle association with the sacred carrying function the Levites performed.
1.literary_structure: The three-verse unit is deliberately compressed: it could have been expanded into a full narrative, but the narrator treats it as a vignette. Its function in the chapter is to establish Samson's undiminished strength before the Delilah narrative erodes it, and to establish the pattern — sleeping in Philistine territory, vulnerable to those who know his location — that Delilah will exploit. The midnight rising and gate-removal is the final display of invulnerability before the cycle turns.
1.theological_application: The Gaza episode illustrates one of the Samson cycle's permanent theological observations: the Spirit's gifts are not cancelled by the recipient's covenant failures. Samson in a prostitute's house in Gaza retains every ounce of the strength the Spirit has given him. The presence of the gift does not certify the covenant integrity of the vessel — an observation the NT makes explicit: 1 Corinthians 13:1–3 warns that prophecy, tongues, knowledge, and miracle-working faith without love are spiritually worthless to the one who exercises them. Samson's gifts served YHWH's purposes (the Philistine conflict) even when Samson's personal life was entirely unworthy of them — which is a description of the gifts' power, not an endorsement of the behavior.
1.christology: The gate of Gaza carried off on Samson's shoulders points toward Matthew 16:18: 'The gates of hell shall not prevail against it.' The city gate was the symbol of power, defense, and judicial authority — the point where a city's sovereignty was exercised. Samson tearing away the gates of the enemy city and depositing them in Israel's territory is the type of what the resurrection accomplishes: the gates of death and Sheol are torn from their posts. Revelation 1:18 applies this imagery directly: 'I have the keys of Death and Hades.' The victor who rises at midnight and walks away with the gates is a faint image of the one who rose at dawn and emptied the tomb.

4.synthesis:
Judges 16:4–22 narrates the Delilah episode in four movements: the Philistine lords' commission, three false disclosures, the fatal fourth disclosure, and the capture and blinding. The narrator opens with a single statement: 'After this he loved a woman in the Valley of Sorek whose name was Delilah' (v. 4). Unlike the chapter 14 wife or the Gaza prostitute, Delilah is named — and named before the lords of the Philistines appear with their offer: 'Seduce him, and see where his great strength lies, and by what means we may overpower him... and we will each give you 1,100 pieces of silver' (v. 5). Five lords at 1,100 pieces each is 5,500 pieces of silver — a staggering sum.

Three times Delilah presses for the secret; three times Samson gives a false answer. Seven fresh bowstrings (v. 7); new ropes (v. 11); weaving the seven locks of his head with the web (v. 13). Each time the Philistines are hidden in the chamber; each time Samson wakes and breaks free. Delilah's fourth approach is the turn: 'How can you say “I love you” when your heart is not with me? You have mocked me these three times and you have not told me where your great strength lies' (v. 15). She presses him every day; 'his soul was vexed to death' (v. 16). He tells her everything: Nazirite from birth, no razor, strength departing if head shaved. Delilah has a man shave his head while he sleeps; he wakes and does not know that YHWH has left him (v. 20). The Philistines seize him, gouge out his eyes, bind him with bronze shackles, and set him grinding grain in the prison at Gaza. The section closes with the note that his hair begins to grow again (v. 22).
4.historical_context: The Sorek Valley — its name deriving from a choice vine — was the same valley where Samson's parents lived at Zorah and where the entire Samson cycle has been set. Delilah is the first woman in the cycle who is not explicitly identified as Philistine, though her cooperation with the Philistine lords makes her allegiances clear. The three false bindings escalate in a pattern: bowstrings → ropes → hair-weaving, each tying Samson's strength to an increasingly central element of his person. The final disclosure — the Nazirite vow and uncut hair — reveals that Samson's strength is not a natural physical property but a covenantal relationship visible in a physical sign. 'He did not know that YHWH had left him' (v. 20) is the cycle's most devastating verse: the judge who had relied on the Spirit's sudden arrivals does not notice when it permanently departs.
4.literary_structure: The fourfold structure of the Delilah episode — three deceptions, one truth — mirrors the chapter 14 wife's seven-day escalation. The narrator handles the three false disclosures quickly (the reader already knows they will fail) and slows down dramatically for the fourth: Delilah's complaint, the daily pressing, the vexation to death, the actual disclosure. The unit ends with the growing hair in v. 22 — a detail that would be irrelevant if the story ended here, but which plants the seed of what the next section will harvest.
4.theological_application: 'He did not know that YHWH had left him' (v. 20) is one of the most sobering verses in the OT — and its most searching application is that Samson did not notice. He went out as at other times and shook himself — assuming the Spirit would be present as it always had been. The permanent theological warning is that the loss of divine presence is not always perceptible at the moment of loss; it may only become apparent in the first moment of testing. The community or individual that has coasted on past Spirit-empowerment without attending to the covenant relationship that sustains it may discover the departure only when strength is needed and does not come. The antidote is not complacency but attentiveness: 1 Thessalonians 5:19 warns not to quench the Spirit; the warning presupposes that quenching can happen gradually and without announcement.
4.christology: Samson's three false answers followed by the truth extracted through persistent pressure echo the pattern of Peter's three denials followed by the triple restoration in John 21:15–17 — though the directions are inverted: Samson's truth leads to capture, Peter's truth leads to restoration. More directly, the Delilah betrayal is the Samson cycle's closest parallel to the Gethsemane narrative: the one who loves, who sleeps in the presence of enemies, who is handed over by someone trusted. Matthew 26:45: 'Sleep and take your rest later on. See, the hour is at hand, and the Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.' The binding, the blinding, the imprisonment — these are the passion sequence in miniature, without the resurrection-yet. The hair that begins to grow in v. 22 is the narrative's first hint that the story is not over.

23.synthesis:
Judges 16:23–31 narrates the death of Samson in the temple of Dagon — the climax of the entire Samson cycle and its most theologically complex moment. The Philistine lords gather to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon their god: 'Our god has given Samson our enemy into our hand' (v. 23). They call for Samson to perform for them; a boy leads the blind Samson in and places him between the two pillars that support the temple, where 'about 3,000 men and women' were present on the roof watching (v. 27). Samson asks the boy to let him feel the pillars — and then prays the most direct prayer in the entire Samson cycle: 'O Lord God, please remember me and please strengthen me only this once, O God, that I may be avenged on the Philistines for my two eyes' (v. 28).

He pushes the two middle pillars — 'the one with his right hand and the other with his left' (v. 29) — and cries: 'Let me die with the Philistines' (v. 30). The temple falls on the lords and all the people. The final verdict of the narrator: 'So the dead whom he killed at his death were more than those he had killed during his life' (v. 30). His brothers and family retrieve his body and bury him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoah his father. He had judged Israel twenty years.
23.historical_context: Dagon was the Philistine grain deity — his temples at Gaza and Ashdod are mentioned in the OT (1 Samuel 5:2). The feast celebrating Samson's capture as Dagon's victory is the Philistines' theological interpretation: their god defeated Israel's judge. Three thousand people on the roof of a temple is plausible given ancient Near Eastern architectural practice — large temples with flat roofs could serve as viewing platforms. The two central pillars supporting such a structure were its structural load points; removing them would cause immediate catastrophic collapse. Samson's prayer for strength 'only this once' (v. 28) is the clearest indication that he knows the Spirit has departed — he asks for its return as a single, final gift, not as a permanent restoration. The Zorah–Eshtaol burial location returns the narrative to its beginning: the territory between these two towns was where the Spirit first stirred Samson (Judges 13:25).
23.literary_structure: The temple scene is structured around the theological irony: the Philistines celebrate Dagon's victory while Samson's hair has been regrowing. The narrative has been waiting since v. 22 for this moment. The pillars Samson feels for provide the structural image of the whole scene: the blind judge, guided by a boy, feeling his way to the two points where everything depends — and then pushing. The symmetry of 'one with his right hand and the other with his left' makes his final act a literal embrace of the temple's structure. The death-count verdict — more killed in death than in life — is the cycle's final word on what kind of judge Samson was: most effective at the moment of greatest weakness and self-surrender.
23.theological_application: Samson's prayer — 'remember me... strengthen me only this once' — is the Samson cycle's purest expression of the theology that has been implicit throughout: all his strength was always gift, never possession. The blinded, imprisoned, humiliated judge knows this more clearly than the Spirit-intoxicated warrior ever did. The permanent application is that weakness can be the condition of the Spirit's most decisive intervention: when Samson's own resources are entirely spent — eyes gone, freedom gone, reputation destroyed — he asks for the gift one more time and receives it. This is the theology Paul will describe in 2 Corinthians 12:9–10: 'My power is made perfect in weakness... when I am weak, then I am strong.' Samson's greatest victory is achieved in his deepest humiliation.
23.christology: Samson's death — arms outstretched between two pillars, body pressed between them, crying 'let me die with the Philistines' — is the OT's most striking anticipation of the crucifixion's form. The arms extended, the voluntary self-giving death, the enemy's mockery just before, the greater victory in death than in life: the pattern is unmistakable. Hebrews 11:32–34 lists Samson among those 'who through faith conquered kingdoms... won strength out of weakness' — and the faith Hebrews has in view is precisely this final prayer, this final act of strength-through-weakness. As Samson's death destroyed more Philistines than his life had, Christ's death destroyed 'the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil' (Hebrews 2:14) — a victory no display of strength during his life had accomplished. The blind judge between two pillars is the OT's most concentrated preview of the cross.
